title: Is dark energy an artifact of decoherence
abstract: Within the quantum Darwinist framework introduced by W. H. Zurek ({\em Nat. Phys.}, 5:181-188, 2009), observers obtain pointer-state information about quantum systems by interacting with the surrounding environment, e.g. the ambient photon field. This framework is applied to the observation of stellar center-of-mass positions, which are assumed to be encoded in a way that is uniformly accessible to all observers regardless of their location. Assuming Landauer's Principle, constructing such environmental encodings requires $\sim$ kT per bit. For 10$^{25}$ stars and a binary encoding of center-of-mass positions into 10 km$^{3}$ voxels, the free energy required at T = 2.7 K is $\sim$ 5 $\cdot$ 10$^{-27}$ kg $\cdot$ m$^{-3}$, in striking agreement with the observed value of $\Omega_{\Lambda} \rho_{c}$. Decreasing the voxel size to $l_{P}^{3}$ results in a free energy requirement 10$^{117}$ times larger.
